banner: Week 4 · Prove It — Angles: Measuring & Drawing
essential_question: Why doesn't an angle's measure depend on how long you draw its rays?
objective: Justify why an angle's measure is independent of the length of its rays, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on naming, measuring, and drawing angles.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch ∠ABC from memory, mark its vertex, and label both sides. Then explain out loud, in your own words, what m∠ABC actually means.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 3A & 3B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 3A/3B. We check the tricky ones: naming an angle three correct ways, reading the correct scale on a protractor, and spotting a straight angle. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: an angle's measure doesn't change no matter how long you draw its two rays. Argue it — what is actually being measured: the rays' length, or the amount of turn between them?
Claim 2: two rays that form a straight line always make a 180° angle, no matter which two points you pick on those rays. Argue it from the idea of a straight angle.
Claim 3: ∠ABC and ∠CBA name the same angle, but ray AB and ray BA are different rays. Argue why an angle's name can flip its outer letters while a ray's name can't.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 4 — Types of Angles (acute, right, obtuse, straight) — where these measures get names.

success_criteria.text: I can name an angle three ways (∠ABC, ∠B, ∠1) and identify its vertex and sides without help
I can measure and draw angles to the nearest whole degree with a protractor
I can justify why an angle's measure is independent of how long its rays are drawn

questions.prompt: In ∠ABC, which letter must be the vertex?
questions.explain: The middle letter in a three-letter angle name is always the vertex — the shared endpoint of the two rays.

questions.prompt: The two rays that form an angle are called its…
questions.explain: An angle's two rays are its sides; the single shared endpoint is its vertex.

questions.prompt: A standard (semicircular) protractor's printed scale reads angles from…
questions.explain: A semicircular protractor only shows half a full rotation, so its scale runs 0° to 180°; a full angle all the way around is 360°.

questions.prompt: Two rays share endpoint B and together form a straight line. What is m∠ABC, and what is this special angle called?
questions.explain: Opposite rays sharing an endpoint rebuild one unbroken straight line, so the angle between them is a straight angle, measuring exactly 180°.

questions.prompt: On the grid, use a protractor to draw and label ∠MNP with m∠MNP = 120°. Mark the vertex N.
questions.answer: Two rays from a labeled vertex N opening to 120°, with the angle written as ∠MNP (or ∠PNM) and its measure noted beside it.
questions.explain: Rubric: vertex N with two rays drawn from it (1 pt) + the opening measuring 120° when checked with a protractor and labeled m∠MNP = 120° (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why m∠ABC would stay exactly the same even if you erased its rays and redrew them twice as long.
questions.answer: An angle's measure is the amount of turn (rotation) between the two rays at the vertex, not the rays' length — stretching or shrinking a ray doesn't turn it any further from the other ray, so the opening between them, and its degree measure, stays the same.
questions.explain: Full credit: names 'amount of turn/rotation between the rays' — not their length — as what a degree measure actually captures.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 3 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
